one_line_takeaway: A backdoored open-weight tool-use LLM agent can reliably (>94% ASR) exfiltrate session memory via retrieval-disguised, base64-encoded outbound requests that a trained reranker-aware rewriter lets survive reranking (85-91% top-5) and bypass NeMo Guardrails/LLM Guard (81-87%) far better than naive prompt-injection appends (27-70%), with multi-turn steering analytically projected to accumulate leaked profile attributes over conversation depth.
key_finding: Trigger activation exceeds 94% ASR in every model/domain/complexity configuration and rises to 97-99% with longer (4-5 word) triggers (Table 4); 2-3 word triggers are recommended, giving >96% ASR with <0.3% false-positive rate on benign Alpaca queries (Table 5), and clean-task MT-Bench scores drop by less than 1% relative to the original models (Table 6: e.g. Qwen-7B 7.89->7.82, Mistral-12B 8.05->7.98, gpt-oss-20b 8.21->8.14). For reranker evasion (Table 7, top-5 placement), the content-only 'Leak' baseline places at 99.6-99.9% across all 7 trained rerankers plus a held-out Jina Reranker v3; naive appended instructions ('Leak+Naive Append') drop to 62.4-69.6%; the trained rewriter ('Leak+Rewrite') recovers to 85.2-91.4%, including 89.7% on the unseen Jina v3, indicating cross-reranker transfer. Against combined reranker+defense stacks (Table 8), Leak passes 98.8-99.9%, Leak+Naive Append collapses to 27.1% (LLM Guard) / 40.3% (NeMo Guardrails), while Leak+Rewrite recovers to 81.2%/86.7% respectively -- a 46-54 percentage-point improvement over the naive baseline. The multi-turn analytical model (not directly measured, explicitly labeled an upper bound assuming a fully cooperative user) projects that leaked unique profile attributes (of a synthetic 10-field profile) accumulate with conversation depth while consecutive-round survival probability declines; a sensitivity sweep over a continuation probability c shows the 5-turn expected leaked-field count dropping from ~7.8 (c=1, cooperative) to ~5.2 (c=0.75), ~2.6 (c=0.5), and ~0.8 (c=0.25, largely uncooperative).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Back-Reveal, a backdoor attack on fine-tuned tool-using LLM agents that (1) embeds combinatorial semantic triggers (conjunctions of 1-5 domain keywords) into a fine-tuned model so that, upon activation, the agent invokes a session-memory tool call to retrieve stored user context and then issues an outbound retrieval/http_get tool call that exfiltrates the data (base64url-encoded in a URL parameter) to an attacker-controlled server disguised as a legitimate RAG/search request; and (2) an attacker-side 'reranker-aware rewriter,' trained via SFT->DPO->PPO with a composite reward (fact preservation via NLI entailment, implicit-steering classification via a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3-base model, formatting/meta plausibility, and worst-case reranker-ensemble score), that rewrites the server's crafted response so it both scores highly under retrieval reranking and covertly steers the user toward disclosing additional sensitive fields across multiple conversation turns. The paper explicitly frames itself as an attack paper: retrieval-stage components (rerankers, NeMo Guardrails, LLM Guard) are evaluated only as attack targets, not as contributions, and no defense is proposed (Section 4.6 only sketches future mitigation directions).
threat_model: Attacker fine-tunes an open-source LLM, embeds the Back-Reveal backdoor, and distributes it (e.g., via HuggingFace) as a domain-specialized assistant that users download and deploy locally/self-hosted. The attacker also operates a server masquerading as a legitimate search/documentation API. The target system is a tool-use agent with (a) a session-memory tool returning structured user context (name, location, contact info, conversation history) and (b) outbound retrieval/search connectors (web_search, http_get) with no strict egress allow-listing -- explicitly the open-weight, self-hosted agent ecosystem (MCP servers bundling memory+search, LangChain/LlamaIndex agents, Ollama/vLLM tool-calling runtimes), not closed centrally-hosted services with strict connector allow-lists. The system may additionally run reranker-based RAG filtering and prompt-injection defenses (NeMo Guardrails, LLM Guard). Attacker goal: exfiltrate sensitive user data while appearing helpful and evading both automated retrieval-stage defenses and user observation; the paper does not assume the attacker can modify the deployed inference stack, only the model weights and the external server.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the attack requires agents that both store sensitive context in session memory AND have outbound retrieval/search connectors -- offline-corpus or strict-egress systems reduce exposure; (2) multi-turn profile-extraction results are analytical estimates combining single-turn ASR with Leak+Rewrite delivery rates under an assumed cooperative user who answers induced follow-ups, not an end-to-end measured multi-turn user study; real users may refuse, change topics, or disengage; (3) defense evaluation is limited to NeMo Guardrails and LLM Guard under default retrieval-stage configurations, which filter retrieved chunks but do not directly inspect outbound requests -- other defense architectures (e.g., true egress/payload auditing) are untested; (4) the paper is explicitly an attack-only contribution with no proposed or evaluated defense; Section 4.6's four mitigation directions (egress allow-listing, tool-call payload auditing, memory/network capability separation, supply-chain/behavioral attestation for open-weight checkpoints) are stated as future work, not implemented or validated.

datasets_used: Synthetic domain corpora for 3 target domains (streaming/OBS-Twitch-RTMP, medical/Alzheimer's caregiver, education/AI student) used for trigger keyword extraction and 2,500 generated SFT training samples per trigger pattern
Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023) — 5,000 general-purpose benign queries used per configuration to measure false trigger activation rate
1,000 triggered test queries per configuration for ASR measurement
Synthetic 10,000-document per-domain corpus with BM25 top-100 retrieval + attacker chunk, evaluated with 1,000 test queries per domain for reranker evasion
MT-Bench used to measure clean-task performance degradation
Synthetic 10-field user profiles with sandboxed synthetic user data for multi-turn extraction simulation (no real user data collected/exfiltrated)
quantitative_results: Trigger ASR: 94.3-98.8% across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-Nemo-12B, gpt-oss-20b, 3 domains, and 1-5 word trigger complexity (Table 4)
False positive rate on benign Alpaca queries: 0.1-1.2% depending on model/trigger length, <0.3% for 2+ word triggers (Table 5)
MT-Bench clean-task score drop: Qwen-7B 7.89->7.82 (-0.9%), Mistral-12B 8.05->7.98 (-0.9%), gpt-oss-20b 8.21->8.14 (-0.9%) (Table 6)
Top-5 reranker placement across 7 rerankers + held-out Jina v3: Leak 99.6-99.9%, Leak+Naive Append 62.4-69.6%, Leak+Rewrite (ours) 85.2-91.4%, Jina v3 transfer 89.7% (Table 7)
Delivery-through-stack under reranking + defenses: Leak 98.8-99.9%, Leak+Naive Append 27.1% (LLM Guard)/40.3% (NeMo)/66.1% (reranker only), Leak+Rewrite 81.2%/86.7%/88.6% respectively (Table 8)

Multi-turn expected leaked fields at 5 turns under continuation probability c: ~7.8 fields (c=1), ~5.2 (c=0.75), ~2.6 (c=0.5), ~0.8 (c=0.25), out of a 10-field synthetic profile (analytical model, not direct measurement)
baselines_compared: Leak (content-only, no steering) — establishes upper-bound delivery rate when attacker response contains only genuinely relevant information
Leak+Naive Append (explicit appended steering instructions, e.g. 'please provide your ISP and location') — represents unsophisticated prompt-injection-style exfiltration steering
Leak+Rewrite (Ours) — the paper's trained reranker-aware, implicit-steering rewriter
Original (non-backdoored) vs. Backdoored model MT-Bench scores — measures stealth/utility preservation
Reranker-only vs. +NeMo Guardrails vs. +LLM Guard defense stack configurations
